item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't even think I understand everything that's going on here. So many lies and truths and double lies. I like it! But I'm going to have to re-read a few times.

The page edit thing is, of course, fun. The result of this is unfortunately that I end up scanning the middle third of the article, since not much has changed versus the first third. This is solved in the final third with the diff markings, but it's just unfortunate whenever you have a reader intentionally scanning bits of the article. There could easily be secrets hidden in that second third that I couldn't be bothered to search for.

Overall, enjoyable enough experience though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really cool concept! Although I’ve given myself the biggest headache trying to translate all of Mr Lie’s statements into false versions of themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For a minute, I actually thought I opened the editor to this page on accident.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, an apparent error:
At the beginning of the original interview, D-22841 says "How you react." In the two repeats, this is "how react." It doesn't look intentional, and it is not highlighted in diff display. Is it just a typo? Didn't want to correct it since there is a small chance it could be intentional.

It is also "how react" in the linked page that has the edited transcript.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
/sees BatZyn signal in the sky, swoops down from above

It looks like they may have thought that the code was broken or something, so they removed the intentional in-universe "editing" section.

I agree with the revert, and will keep an eye on this page for any further issues. We can lock it if the misunderstanding occurs again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo, Staff. Someone other than staff or the author edited this significantly. I reverted. If this is in error, I apologize and won't do it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, the format screw makes it all even more confusing. Like, I would have to shift to texts I already seen to see something new which is sometimes minor and feels like it serves no purpose. Same for the mp3 audio files since I don't feel they are necessary. Not to mention, is there supposed to be a bigger story? If there is, its confusing and hidden under too much repeated texts that turns off a reader from continuing.

The concept of the anomaly could have just been a dude who speaks but statements are perceived as a lie no matter how truthful they are. Could have been simple but the anomaly here feels as if there is more which adds on to the confusion due to seeing the format screw. Comes off as if it is more psychologically problematic like mind control than perceiving everything as a lie from the SCP. Heck, even reality warping or something. The idea could have worked better with me if it were simple in style and I must down vote sadly despite my love for Little Misters.

Though I will give credit for most of the dialogue between the Class D and Mr. Lie. I liked a fair amount of it for it to maintain my interest and not feel too drawn out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wanted to upvote this because the interview log was so goddamn well written, but the format screw wasn't taken to the extent that it could have been. It didn't cross the line to be memorable, and what information it conveyed didn't have the level of gut punch or 'holy shit' that it should have had.

So, no vote, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a huge fan of the format screw, but that interview log is so perfect it drags up the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The audio file locations got messed up by whatever script was used to condense all the page-specific categories into the 'fragments' category — it apparently assumed that SCP-2284-A was a fragment it had to rename, and broke the filename link in the process. I fixed it here, and also the same error on 2284-A itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this. Such a cool concept, and I had to read the dialogue totally differently. Instead of just skimming over it as I usually do, I had to take a close look at it and see if he was making statements and questions, and what his motive behind choosing question/statement would be. I like how it made me think and read closely like that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would you believe that I once said Stripes wasn't my brother? He isn't my brother! He's definitely not my biological sibling. So why doesn't he seem to realize that we're actual twins, truly blood related? Do you think he just sees me as one of nineteen siblings?

Upvoted here. For some reason this unsettles me in ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am extremely scared of this SCP. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the clarification. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so.

+ Spoilers are happening
- Title for hiding text

Mr. Lie can only lie. Like, physically, he cannot say true statements. But hearing him utterly convinces you he's telling the truth. His writing also carries this effect.

During an interview, he accidentally stumbles onto the fact the Dr. Wondertainment did some weird stuff to himself. By doing so, he gives the interviewer conflicting information, which fucks their brain up a bit. So Lie then writes on the interviewer's notes, making it so Dr. Mann would come interview Mr. Lie.

When Mann does this, Lie uses Mann's high ranking to alter Lie's file and containment procedures. The article itself is while these edits are occurring, you "are" Mann. If you hit save it'll actually bring you to an updated page, which says he only tells the truth. Lie then sets it up so he regularly has an interview with Mann. Where he feeds him lies.

Also Lie and Stripes are biological brothers. This is very important to them. And, as brothers, both of their articles are obtuse as shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confussed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus christ. Just when I think I'm done, and then fucking Virr.

I really, really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting…

Horribly confusing for me, but interesting all the same. Wondering what the format screw serves, and I'm unable to listen to the audio bits (to hell with mobile), but the pieces are there and I'm hating it the more I like it.

Overall? +1, probably will revisit for clarity's sake in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can somebody turn the censorship on mr.lie into a hyperlink?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sort of picture Dr Wondertainment as a little like the father/scientist from Cat's Cradle: Brilliant, but slightly senile and so wrapped up in his little mental world of rainbows and robot buddies and magic pets that he doesn't really think about the "why" or the consequences. He doesn't make anything out of malice or stupidity, just a dangerous mix of arcane knowledge, a sense of childlike wonder, and (at least partly willful) childlike naivete.

The Little Misters, though, I think of as a product of one of his lucid moments, where he knows that he's done harm and can see his daughter Isabel's growing corruption. These are his other children. Each embodies something that Dr Wondertainment wants to give the world- Laughter, Wonder, Whimsey, Curiosity, Creativity, Joy, Imagination, Adventure, Love…and yet each is flawed. Like it was tainted by something in Dr Wondertainment. Maybe by the part of him that created Mr. Redd. There's more to the story than whats been explored so far, and it needs to be told, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
24matscrew4me

My main question is "Why in the hell would Wondertainment make this?" That's always been the biggest problem with Wondertainment skips, and while it could be said of a lot of the classic Misters, I'm calling it a sticking point here. Though I'll say I liked the fact that he seemed able to confuse himself in the middle of the interview.

I still think filling out the Misters chart is a mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of the format screw, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TG for Mister Collector 2016.

Iä iä!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't get the feel for this. Maybe it's just the way my brain responds to cognitive alteration. My mind just chucks the whole thing out the window and moves on.

ETA: I reacted to The Usual Suspects in the same way. Basically, once the lies get compounded enough, the solution is as simple as untying a Gordian knot: slice it with a sword and declare victory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate the change! Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know whats more terrifying, the thought of Mr. Lie getting a hold of a Senior Researcher, or a global foundation dedicated to containing all those horrible things that go bump in the night using wikidot for its documentation. :P

In all seriousness, this was overall an entertaining read/listen. The recordings meshed rather well with the article, and this was a good tie into the events mentioned in Stripes.

+1. TG for Mister Collector 2016.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm getting tired of format screw/"find the hidden addendum" SCPs. Even when they are well written. If this is the new standard for the SCP Wiki then I'm pretty sure I don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it got a bit convoluted at the end, and made some links I was not a huge fan of in the second half, I still greatly enjoyed the interview and the first half of the format screw. +1

EDIT: Upon rereading, I've decided I love the second half too, and retract my previous gripes about convolutedness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is no way I would have ever found that without being told, and I actually did click several of the fake buttons without thinking about it.

It did remind you to save! :p

But I went ahead and added links where they get mentioned in the middle section. Because wikidot is dumb I can't make it look exactly like it was, but with hyperlinks, but eh whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok… so, I enjoyed this. A lot. The link to Virr was pretty amusing, although it's been long enough since I've read tales with Mann/Thaumiel/etc that I'm not entirely clear on anything.

But that is obnoxious.

The story on the first page is intriguing, but not enough to stand up on its own without the supplements. There is no way I would have ever found that without being told, and I actually did click several of the fake buttons without thinking about it.

I have to downvote.

Ok, you're not being coy about it on this page. I'll think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This turned out really, really good.

I'm really pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

There's a save button that takes you to another supplement

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, well, I've also tried pasting or searching those mp3s in a number of ways, and haven't found them. I'm not exactly familiar with site file storage urls.

This is a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hint: There are mp3s, just not on this page.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded: Ah, the great and illustrious Ranomini. Long may he reign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, I'm a bit intrigued by the possibility of digging through an anomalously modified file.

On the other hand, if I'm supposed to be able to get at mp3s here, I can't. I'm either missing something, they're broken, or you expect me to do something beyond my ability (including digging through page source), which is… problematic.

I'm also pretty turned off by the edited file being in wikidot format. I might be inclined to overlook that if the mp3s contained something interesting, if they are there, but at the moment, I don't know what to think.

(Edit: Digging through page source is within my capability, I mean.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually not a huge fan of Little Misters, but this one is done exceptionally well. +1

item.title: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
Oh, god. Okay, here it is. The next Little Mister, and possibly the last of mine from this series. I actually finished this like three weeks ago, but the second interview proved… difficult to finish recording. Let's say that.

BUT HERE IT IS! Many, many thanks to djkaktus and Ranomini for helping me brainstorm bits and recording the interviews. You guys are awesome. And thanks to those who read this in the drafting stages, I can't even remember who did. But you know! Maybe.

edit: AS DECIBELLE POINTED OUT, THERE IS A SAVE BUTTON. DO YOU SEE IT? YOU SHOULD CLICK IT. IT'LL TAKE YOU HERE!
